title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a baker called Karen talking about a community bakery training project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a baker called Karen talking about a community bakery training project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A baker called Karen explains a community bakery training project.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. I’m Karen and I manage Morning Rise, a community bakery where volunteers learn practical skills while producing food for people in the area. The bakery began in a school kitchen during holidays, but this meant we could not operate throughout the year. Three years ago we moved into a converted [9]church hall[/9], where a modern kitchen was installed at one end and tables for training were placed at the other. We are now open four mornings a week and have volunteers ranging from secondary school students to retired people.||Not everything we bake is sold. A small number of cakes are available at our counter to cover ingredient costs, while part of each day’s baking is provided to community organisations. Customers often ask for pastries, but the item we make in the greatest quantities is [10]bread rolls[/10], because they can be used for lunches and are easy to transport. People joining us do not need experience. However, before working in the kitchen, all new volunteers complete training in [11]food hygiene[/11], learning about clean surfaces, handwashing and safe storage.||On the first practical morning, beginners do not use ovens and are not expected to decorate cakes. Their initial task is usually [12]weighing ingredients[/12], following simple written instructions so that experienced bakers can prepare dough efficiently. All equipment is supplied. Volunteers must wear sensible closed shoes, and we give everyone a clean [13]apron[/13] when they arrive, which remains at the bakery to be washed afterwards. Hair must also be tied back or covered before food preparation begins. This may sound strict, but we have a responsibility to the people receiving our food.||At the end of each morning, completed products are cooled and packed. We used to carry them in ordinary bags, which sometimes damaged the bread. They are now placed in reusable [14]delivery boxes[/14], each clearly labelled before leaving the kitchen. Although we support a number of local groups, the organisation receiving our largest weekly delivery is a [15]homeless shelter[/15], which uses the bread for breakfasts and packed meals. Volunteers are sometimes invited to visit so they can understand how their work helps. We offer extra classes too. Last year's most popular lesson involved making pizza; this term, places filled fastest for our [16]cake decorating[/16] session.||The project aims to increase confidence as well as teach practical skills. Each volunteer who completes eight mornings is given a [17]recipe booklet[/17], containing the products they have helped make and advice on baking them at home. It is a useful reminder of their progress. Finally, we need to raise funds for another mixer, since our present equipment is in constant use. We considered a formal dinner but decided it would be costly. Instead, local families will be invited to our [18]breakfast fair[/18] in July, where fresh bread, fruit and hot drinks will be served. Anyone interested in helping can add their name to the list after this talk.

dialogue.speaker: karen
dialogue.text: Good morning. I’m Karen and I manage Morning Rise, a community bakery where volunteers learn practical skills while producing food for people in the area. The bakery began in a school kitchen during holidays, but this meant we could not operate throughout the year. Three years ago we moved into a converted church hall, where a modern kitchen was installed at one end and tables for training were placed at the other. We are now open four mornings a week and have volunteers ranging from secondary school students to retired people.||Not everything we bake is sold. A small number of cakes are available at our counter to cover ingredient costs, while part of each day’s baking is provided to community organisations. Customers often ask for pastries, but the item we make in the greatest quantities is bread rolls, because they can be used for lunches and are easy to transport. People joining us do not need experience. However, before working in the kitchen, all new volunteers complete training in food hygiene, learning about clean surfaces, handwashing and safe storage.||On the first practical morning, beginners do not use ovens and are not expected to decorate cakes. Their initial task is usually weighing ingredients, following simple written instructions so that experienced bakers can prepare dough efficiently. All equipment is supplied. Volunteers must wear sensible closed shoes, and we give everyone a clean apron when they arrive, which remains at the bakery to be washed afterwards. Hair must also be tied back or covered before food preparation begins. This may sound strict, but we have a responsibility to the people receiving our food.||At the end of each morning, completed products are cooled and packed. We used to carry them in ordinary bags, which sometimes damaged the bread. They are now placed in reusable delivery boxes, each clearly labelled before leaving the kitchen. Although we support a number of local groups, the organisation receiving our largest weekly delivery is a homeless shelter, which uses the bread for breakfasts and packed meals. Volunteers are sometimes invited to visit so they can understand how their work helps. We offer extra classes too. Last year's most popular lesson involved making pizza; this term, places filled fastest for our cake decorating session.||The project aims to increase confidence as well as teach practical skills. Each volunteer who completes eight mornings is given a recipe booklet, containing the products they have helped make and advice on baking them at home. It is a useful reminder of their progress. Finally, we need to raise funds for another mixer, since our present equipment is in constant use. We considered a formal dinner but decided it would be costly. Instead, local families will be invited to our breakfast fair in July, where fresh bread, fruit and hot drinks will be served. Anyone interested in helping can add their name to the list after this talk.
